overview: Converts all line-breaks in a text-based input document to a specified type (DOS/Windows, Unix/Linux, or Macintosh). The input line-break type is detected automatically and may contain a mix of different types. Operates on raw documents and preserves encoding and Byte-Order-Marks.

lineBreak.help:
Controls which line-break sequence is written to the output file. The input file's line-break types are detected automatically and may be mixed — all are converted to the selected type regardless of their original form.

Available values:
- **DOS/Windows** — `\r\n` (CR+LF, `0x0D 0x0A`)
- **Unix/Linux** — `\n` (LF, `0x0A`)
- **Macintosh** — `\r` (CR, `0x0D`)
lineBreak.values: Encoded line-break strings: `$0d$$0a$` for DOS/Windows (CR+LF), `$0a$` for Unix/Linux (LF), `$0d$` for Macintosh (CR).
notes: The input encoding must be specified explicitly, even for formats that usually auto-detect encoding (XML, HTML, etc.). The only exception is files with a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM).
Output encoding matches the input encoding. BOMs are preserved if present.

processingNotes: Input encoding must be specified explicitly even for self-describing formats (XML, HTML). Only BOM-prefixed files are exempt from this requirement.
Output is written in the same encoding as the input document.
Byte-Order-Marks are preserved if present in the input.
The step auto-detects the input line-break type and handles mixed line-break styles within a single file.

examples.title: Convert a Windows file to Unix line endings
examples.description: Convert a DOS/Windows text file (`\r\n`) to Unix/Linux line endings (`\n`) for use in Linux environments.
